data.title: Sally Beauty targets $120M in cumulative cost savings by 2026 while launching Sally Ignited store refresh
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sally Beauty Holdings, Inc. (SBH) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Denise Paulonis, President, CEO & Director, announced, "Fiscal 2025 was a meaningful year for the company, highlighted by strong operating and financial performance in the context of a rapidly changing and uncertain macro environment." She emphasized comparable sales growth of 1.3%, a 100 basis point gross margin expansion to 52.2%, an adjusted operating margin of 9.4%, and a 10% increase in adjusted diluted earnings per share to $0.55 for Q4. Full year adjusted diluted EPS reached $1.90, up 12%.
  • Paulonis highlighted a $275 million operating cash flow, $119 million debt paydown, and over $50 million in share repurchases. She also noted the successful launch of Sally Ignited, a comprehensive brand refresh, and the expansion of the marketplace strategy, including a new partnership with Uber Eats.
  • Key product category growth was led by color, with 7% growth in Q4 and 4% for the full year, and digital sales momentum, with Sally U.S. and Canada's e-commerce sales up 34% year-over-year in Q4. BSG segment saw innovation drive about 30% of total hair care sales in 2025.
  • Strategic priorities for 2026 include customer activation, digital value, product innovation, and expanding new growth pathways. The CEO stated, "Our mission is to ensure that the Sally brand emotionally connects with our customers while creating a discovery-focused omnichannel specialty beauty experience."
  • Marlo Cormier, Senior VP & CFO, stated, "We concluded the year with strong business momentum, enabling us to deliver fourth quarter and full year results ahead of our expectations on the top and bottom line. Our performance reflects our disciplined execution and commitment to long-term value creation."

Outlook

  • For fiscal 2026, Sally Beauty expects consolidated net sales between $3.71 billion and $3.77 billion, comparable sales flat to up 1%, adjusted operating earnings of $328 million to $342 million, and adjusted diluted EPS in the range of $2 to $2.10 per share. The company plans capital expenditures of about $100 million and free cash flow of approximately $200 million.
  • Q1 2026 guidance includes consolidated net sales of $935 million to $945 million, comparable sales approximately flat, adjusted operating earnings of $75 million to $80 million, and adjusted diluted EPS between $0.43 and $0.47.
  • Paulonis shared a three-year target through 2028: net sales growth of 1% to 3% annually, adjusted operating earnings growth of 3% to 5%, adjusted diluted EPS growth of at least 10%, and $90 million to $120 million in annual capital expenditures.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated net sales for Q4 increased 1.3% to $947 million. Comparable sales rose 1.3%. Gross margin expanded 100 basis points to 52.2%. Adjusted SG&A for Q4 was $405 million, up $14 million year-over-year.
  • Global e-commerce sales increased 15% to $105 million, representing 11% of total net sales. Sally Beauty segment net sales grew 1.4% to $542 million, with comparable sales up 1.2%. Sally e-commerce sales rose 23% to $47 million. BSG segment net sales increased 1.1% to $406 million, with comparable sales up 1.4%.
  • Inventory at year-end was $988 million, down 5% from last year. Cash and cash equivalents totaled $149 million, with no outstanding borrowings under the revolver. Free cash flow for Q4 was $78 million.
  • The Fuel for Growth program delivered $46 million in incremental pretax benefits in 2025, with a cumulative run rate of $74 million since inception and a target of $120 million by end of 2026.

Q&A

  • Oliver Chen, TD Cowen, Research Division: Asked about divisional performance drivers, comp ticket trends, and guidance regarding occupancy leverage. Paulonis responded that color strength in both divisions was notable, with Sally color up 8% and BSG up 5%, and discussed customer activation and ongoing positive momentum. Cormier addressed occupancy leverage, expecting similar leverage to last year.
  • Chen inquired about the consumer environment, possible comp upside, and long-term growth leverage points. Paulonis cited resilience in Sally customers and stability in stylists, noting "consumers remain choiceful" and monitoring lower-income segments affected by government shutdowns. Cormier stated Fuel for Growth will continue to provide leverage.
  • Susan Anderson, Canaccord Genuity Corp.: Asked for an update on the Sally store remodel program. Paulonis explained that 30 stores had been refreshed, with higher dwell time and cross-category shopping. Fifty more stores are planned for fiscal 2026.
  • Anderson followed up on growth in color at Sally and customer behavior. Paulonis highlighted growth from new, reactivated, and existing customers, with Licensed Colorist OnDemand driving confidence and store visits.
  • Lauren (for Simeon Gutman), Morgan Stanley: Asked about confidence in long-term EBIT growth as Fuel for Growth winds down. Cormier pointed to ongoing supply chain optimization and own brand penetration.
  • Lauren asked about Sally segment traffic and ticket expectations for 2026. Paulonis projected improvements driven by performance marketing, personalization, and cross-category shopping.
  • Sydney Wagner, Jefferies: Asked about category growth trends underpinning long-term sales targets and promotional activity. Paulonis expects color to remain core, with nail and care gaining traction at Sally and innovation driving BSG. Promotions at Sally were more frequent but shorter.
  • Olivia Tong Cheang, Raymond James: Sought details on category growth assumptions and Q1 headwinds. Paulonis cited expected pressure on lower-income customers in Q1 from the government shutdown but confidence in the full-year outlook. She also discussed supporting stylists through improved app functionality for on-demand purchases.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on divisional performance drivers, store remodel success, and long-term growth levers, while expressing some caution regarding consumer pressure and promotional dynamics.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident and detailed, with Paulonis stating, "we are pleased to finish the year strong and look forward to making meaningful progress in fiscal 2026 towards the long-term financial targets." In Q&A, management remained constructive but acknowledged near-term headwinds and maintained prudence regarding guidance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone continued to be confident, reinforced by stronger operating results and positive momentum in strategic initiatives. Analyst sentiment remained focused on key performance risks but recognized the progress on innovation and digital strategy.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 guidance introduced a three-year financial target, offering more visibility than the prior quarter's focus on annual guidance. Management expanded on the Sally Ignited brand refresh, now covering 30 stores compared to 20 last quarter.
  • Growth in the color category accelerated to 7% in Q4 from 4% in Q3, with digital marketplace expansion also gaining momentum.
  • Management continued to credit the Fuel for Growth program with driving cost savings, now reporting $74 million in cumulative run rate benefits, up from $70 million last quarter.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from concerns over macro-driven softness in core categories to the impact of government shutdowns and the efficacy of new store formats.
  • Management's confidence was reinforced by improved operating performance, while analysts remained cautious about the consumer environment and the sustainability of growth drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited government shutdowns as a factor affecting lower-income consumer traffic in Q1 2026 and remains watchful of ongoing macroeconomic pressures.
  • Supply chain and cost of goods impacts from tariff increases were noted, with mitigation planned through vendor negotiations, sourcing optimization, and modest price increases.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of growth as Fuel for Growth savings begin to wind down and whether category and digital innovation can continue to drive traffic and sales.

Final Takeaway

Sally Beauty Holdings closed fiscal 2025 with solid sales growth, margin expansion, and strong cash flow, underpinned by strategic investments in store refreshes, digital innovation, and product assortment. The company enters 2026 with clear growth drivers—customer activation, digital value, and innovation—supported by the ongoing Fuel for Growth program targeting $120 million in cumulative savings. Management remains confident in its long-term growth trajectory, while acknowledging near-term risks from macroeconomic factors and a cautious consumer environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Flux Power outlines $1B market expansion and cost cuts while advancing software and product certifications
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Flux Power Holdings, Inc. (FLUX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Krishna Vanka, CEO, reported that revenue in the quarter reflected a temporary pause in customer orders "mainly due to the uncertainty surrounding the tariff situation during the quarter and also due to the near-term caution regarding the macroeconomic situation." Vanka highlighted a rebound in order activity in the second fiscal quarter, citing multimillion dollar orders totaling $2.4 million from top material handling customers and a large new order from a major airline. He noted that "we now supply to 8 major North American airlines, and this represents doubling of our airline customer base compared to last year."
  • Vanka outlined five strategic initiatives: profitable growth, operational efficiencies, solution selling, building the right products, and integrating value-added software for recurring revenue. He stated, "Since my arrival, we have reduced our headcount costs by a total of 20% while maintaining consistent production levels."
  • The CEO announced completion of two capital raises totaling $13.8 million in net proceeds, with funds "efficiently used for working capital needs and to accelerate our product development road map."
  • Vanka revealed that the company received UL EE listing across the entire material handling portfolio, opening new market segments "representing around $1 billion in total addressable market," and achieved UL 1973 listing for 80-volt intelligent batteries. He reported a certification from a major OEM for new lift truck models.
  • Vanka described progress on software, stating, "we graduated our SkyEMS 2.0 SaaS platform and converted a major airline from beta testing to a paying customer."
  • Kevin Royal, CFO, stated, "Revenue for the fiscal first quarter of 2026 was $13.2 million compared to $16.1 million in the same quarter last year.... Gross margin in the first quarter was 28.6% compared to 32.4% in the prior year period."
  • Royal added, "Operating expenses in the first quarter of 2026 were $5.9 million compared to $6.4 million in the first quarter of 2025.... The net loss for the first quarter was $2.6 million or $0.15 per share compared to a net loss of $1.7 million or $0.10 per share in the first quarter of 2025."

Outlook

  • Vanka shared that "order activity rebound in our second fiscal quarter" is highlighted by recent orders and that the company now serves double the number of airline customers compared to last year. He also indicated ongoing work towards full cloud connectivity for all batteries and further AI-driven features for the SkyEMS platform, with details promised in future calls.
  • CFO Royal noted, "we are seeing more promising trends in the second half of the year and in particular, seeing some strengthening in our third fiscal quarter, which is the first calendar quarter of 2026."

Financial Results

  • CFO Royal reported revenue of $13.2 million for Q1 2026 and gross margin of 28.6%. Operating expenses were $5.9 million. Net loss was $2.6 million or $0.15 per share. Non-GAAP net loss was $2.4 million or $0.14 per share. Adjusted EBITDA was negative $1.7 million.
  • He confirmed cash and cash equivalents at quarter end were $1.6 million. Post-quarter, the company raised $9.2 million from a secondary offering and $4.6 million from a private placement, aimed at working capital and accelerating product redesign.

Q&A

  • Robert Brown, Lake Street Capital Markets, LLC: "First question on kind of the order trends sort of post quarter... are you seeing that strength continue into the fourth quarter?" Royal answered, "we are seeing some evidence of a rebound... we really are still seeing some headwinds, which we continue to attribute to recent tariffs as well as some impact in the quarter from the government shutdown. However, we are seeing more promising trends in the second half of the year."
  • Brown: "On the ground support equipment market, you've had good progress there... what sort of further penetration can you get there?" Vanka replied, "They continue to adopt the clean energy solutions in the GSE. So I'm not seeing any pushback from the overall goal... It was really this short-term tariff that paused some of the progress."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on order recovery and market penetration, but highlighted concerns about macroeconomic and tariff headwinds.
  • Management maintained a constructive and confident tone, emphasizing progress on cost reductions, capital raises, certifications, and product development, while acknowledging short-term challenges. Vanka stated, "despite the challenges we faced during the quarter, I'm really proud of the progress we have made."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone has shifted slightly more cautious due to the pause in orders and margin compression, but remains forward-looking and focused on recovery.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Q1 2026 call highlighted a revenue drop and margin compression compared to Q4 2025, which saw year-over-year growth and gross margin expansion. The current quarter focused on order delays due to tariffs, while the previous quarter highlighted increased quoting activity and optimism for backlog growth.
  • Strategic priorities remain consistent: cost reductions, expansion into new verticals, and emphasis on software and recurring revenue, but the current quarter features more updates on certifications, capital raises, and new customer wins.
  • Analysts shifted focus from gross margin improvement and software rollout in Q4 2025 to order flow recovery and macroeconomic risks in Q1 2026.
  • Management’s confidence remains steady, with a slightly more defensive posture regarding external headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "uncertainty surrounding the tariff situation" and macroeconomic caution as primary reasons for the pause in orders and margin impact.
  • Headwinds from tariffs and a government shutdown continue to affect order flow, though management noted a rebound is emerging.
  • Operating margins were pressured by a shift in product mix to lower-margin items.

Final Takeaway

Flux Power leadership emphasized that despite near-term order delays and margin pressure due to tariffs and macroeconomic caution, the company remains focused on operational efficiencies, accelerating product development, and expanding into new market segments. Recent certifications, capital raises, and growth in the airline customer base are seen as key drivers positioning the company for improved performance and profitable growth as order activity rebounds in upcoming quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gold turns lower, part of broad market selloff following U.S. government reopen
data.description:
Gold and silver futures fell modestly Thursday on profit-taking pressure following recent strong gains that lifted silver to an all-time record and three-week highs for gold.

Analysts said precious metals were caught in a classic buy-the-rumor, sell-the-news trade after the House and Senate voted to reopen the U.S. government, with stocks, bonds, the dollar and crypto all falling.

Gold and silver rose initially on expectations that economic data released after the end of the shutdown will reveal U.S. labor market weakness and push the Federal Reserve toward at least one December rate cut, Kitco senior analyst Jim Wyckoff said in a note, but a growing number of Fed policymakers are signaling a reluctance to further easing, citing worries about inflation and signs of relative stability in the labor market.

San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, previously a firm supporter of the Fed's rate cuts, said Thursday that any decision ahead of the next policy meeting is "premature," and Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari, who once believed a third rate cut by year-end would be warranted, told Bloomberg that he had opposed last month's rate cut because of resilience in the economy, and is on the fence about December.

"Markets bet that once data flows restart, a softer U.S. economic picture could justify additional rate cuts, which support the non-yielding metal, [but] divisions within the Fed persist, with some policymakers favoring a pause to curb inflation," MUFG's Soojin Kim said in a note.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for November delivery finished -0.4% to $4,186.90/oz, and front-month Comex November silver (XAGUSD:CUR) settled -0.5% to $53.074/oz, still the metal's second-highest close in history.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Under Armour plans to separate Curry Brand as part of its expanded restructuring plan
data.description:
Under Armour (UAA) said on Thursday that the company and basketball star Stephen Curry have planned to separate Curry Brand from Under Armour. 

Under Armour will develop new UA Basketball products and continue to support athletes and programs across every level of the game, the company said. 

Under the separation, Curry will become independent of Under Armour. UA will release the Curry 13—the final Curry Brand x Under Armour shoe—in February 2026 as planned, with additional colorways and apparel collections available through October 2026.

Under Armour (UAA) also announced the expansion of its previously disclosed fiscal 2025 restructuring plan and increased its fiscal 2026 adjusted operating income outlook.

Previously, the company anticipated incurring up to $160M in pre-tax restructuring and related charges in connection with its fiscal 2025 restructuring plan. Following further review, Under Armour's board has approved an additional $95M in restructuring actions, the primary benefits of which will be realized in future periods. 

This includes the separation of the Curry Brand from Under Armour, further contract terminations, incremental asset impairments, and additional employee severance and benefits costs.

The company estimates that its total global basketball business, including Curry Brand, will approximate $100M to $120M in revenue for fiscal 2026. In connection with the separation of the Curry Brand, the company does not anticipate a significant effect on its consolidated financial results or profitability.

The expansion of the restructuring and transformation plan brings the total estimated restructuring and related charges to up to $255M. 

As of September 30, 2025, Under Armour had incurred approximately $147M of restructuring and related charges. 

Under Armour also raised its fiscal 2026 adjusted operating income outlook. 

On a GAAP-basis, the company now expects an operating loss of $56M to $71M versus its previous expectation of operating income of $19M to $34M. Adjusted operating income is now expected to reach $95M to $110M, compared to the prior range of $90M to $105M.

data.title: Seth Klarman's Baupost takes stake in Union Pacific, exits ViaSat, among Q3 trades
data.description:
Baupost acquired new stakes in Union Pacific (UNP), boosted holdings in Restaurant Brands (QSR), and reduced its holdings in Alphabet C shares (GOOG), according to its latest 13F filing on Thursday.

The hedge fund took new stakes in Union Pacific (UNP) with ~1.50M shares valued at $353.7M, Genuine Parts (GPC) with ~1.40M shares valued at $193.3M, and Americold (COLD) with 3.6M shares valued at $44.1M.

Baupost increased stakes in Restaurant Brands International (QSR) to 8.25M shares (value $529.3M) from 4.05M, Elevance Health (ELV) to 1.32M shares (value $426.2M) from 616K, and Eagle Materials (EXP) to 939.7K shares (value $219.0M) from 676.3K.

The firm exited Icon Plc (ICLR) and ViaSat (VSAT). It reduced its takes in Alphabet (GOOG) to 1.86M shares (value $452.6M) from 2.63M, Wesco International (WCC) to 1.50M shares (value $317.9M) from ~2.21M shares and Liberty Global class C shares (LBTYK) to 21.7M shares (value $254.5M) from ~24.7M shares, according to the filing.
